days.notes:
**Title**
Toward Fisterra, alone: long stage and atmosphere shift.

**Where I was / stage**
July 19: departure from Santiago on Camino de Fisterra, arrival in Vilaserío.

**Key scene**
July 19 was the day I truly felt the phase change. Leaving Santiago, I resumed walking westward with a very long stage: on paper it looked like about 30/32 km, but on the phone it came out almost 38.5. I arrived in Vilaserío exhausted.

Physical fatigue was evident, but the strongest difference was in the atmosphere: this was no longer the Camino Francés I had lived for weeks. On Camino de Fisterra I found new people, often already in pairs or small groups, and less of that spontaneous continuous-encounter magic I had known before. It felt a bit like returning to the first days: you walk, you greet, but you feel the context is different.

Meanwhile the “old group” was dissolving definitively: Francesco and Andrius were already in return mode, with flights and transfers underway. We updated each other at distance, and even there the same thing was perceptible: the Camino had changed rhythm for everyone.

With Catherine we talked throughout the day. I told her about the stage, the tiredness, and this sensation of “less magic.” She, from her return to family life, was living a similar passage: less trail, more everyday reality. It was one of those dialogues where you understand the emotional side of returning has already started, even if you are still walking.

In the evening, after shower, dinner, and laundry, I was finally on the albergue bed with an emptied body. But inside, the meaning of the day stayed clear: I was still moving forward, just in a different way.

**Sensory detail**
Heavy legs, dry sweat, backpack pulling on shoulders, and that mechanical final-hour stride when you move forward mostly by inertia.

**What I understood**
It was also the day I had focused the symbolic plan for the ending: I would run Muxía-Finisterre as the final day of the Camino and as a mental bridge toward the new USA adventure planned for November.

Not all final stretches have the same magic as the first ones, but right there you learn the hardest part of the Camino: continue even when enthusiasm drops, and stay faithful to the direction you chose.

**Title**
Toward Muxía, with nostalgia already in the air.

**Where I was / stage**
July 20: from Dumbría toward Muxía, with a plan already set to then close on Finisterre.

**Key scene**
July 20 was a transition day that already contained the whole ending. I was walking toward Muxía with the sensation that the “classic” part of the Camino was now behind me, and that from there on every choice was more free, more personal.

In the group, post-Camino nostalgia was strongly in the air: messages about car returns, airports, memories suddenly resurfacing while reading town signs we had crossed together. It was evident that for everyone that strange phase was starting in which the body is already elsewhere, but the mind is still on the trail.

Meanwhile I was designing my ending: arrive in Muxía the next day, then Finisterre the day after, ideally from km 0.000 to km 0.000, and possibly running. With Carla we discussed exactly this: she gave me practical tips on timing and fog at the sanctuary, and in that moment the plan took concrete shape.

During the day I also heard from Catherine: she was with her family and told me about a very different dimension from mine, more domestic and less free, while I was still immersed in Camino rhythm. At the end of the day it was also beautiful to find familiar faces again: Sara and Renato in the same albergue in Dumbría. In that phase, every familiar encounter had the taste of a small gift.

**Sensory detail**
Atlantic air getting closer and closer, moving sky, and that coastal light that changes fast and makes you understand you are near the far western edge.

**What I understood**
In the final days it is not only about “arriving”: it is about how you choose to close the circle, and with what energy you want to carry the whole Camino back home.

**Title**
Muxía, the edge of the world and an earned sunset.

**Where I was / stage**
July 21: full day in Muxía, between symbolic arrival, sea, and preparation for the finale toward Finisterre/Santiago.

**Key scene**
July 21 was one of those days that do not need big numbers to stay impressed. I was in Muxía, finally with my own “arrival” photo, and in the group there was a beautiful climate: crossed congratulations, jokes, stories from those already back home who still kept feeling part of the same Camino family.

During the day I saw Giselle again: we met in the morning and planned to meet again in the evening, with the idea of closing the day in front of the sunset. In between, the rhythm was the right one for Muxía: sea, long pauses, photos, and that end-of-journey sensation in which every detail seems to carry more weight than usual.

With Catherine we talked often: I sent her images of the place, she wrote that she wished she could be there. There was physical distance, but still a very alive connection, made of simple and sincere messages. Meanwhile I was also beginning to prepare the next step: the day after I would aim for Finisterre, to complete the “double finale” I had set in my mind.

The perfect closure came in the evening: a clean, full sunset, the kind that after weeks of Camino you almost feel as a reward. There I had the clear perception that the journey was changing phase: less conquest, more absorption of everything that had happened.

**Sensory detail**
Coastal wind, smell of salt, warm light slowly dropping over the sea and forcing even your thoughts to slow down.

**What I understood**
When you reach symbolic places like Muxía, it is not only about “having arrived there”: it is about the state of mind with which you arrive, and with whom you choose to share that moment.

**Title**
From Muxía to Finisterre: the hardest and truest ending.

**Where I was / stage**
July 22: from km 0.000 in Muxía to Finisterre, largely running, with closure at the lighthouse.

**Key scene**
On July 22 I set myself a clear mission: start from km 0.000 in Muxía and reach km 0.000 in Finisterre running. At 11:00 I really started. The idea was great, but the day was very hard: heat, heavy stretches, energy rising and falling, continuous rhythm changes between running and walking.

When I arrived in Finisterre I was cooked. I felt sick, I also vomited, and for hours I could hardly eat anything: only water, lots of water, and little else. After a shower and a bit of recovery I still decided to close the circle completely: I went to the lighthouse, another 3 km, for the passage at marker 0.000. It was the most exhausting part of the day, done practically like a zombie, but it was also the part I wanted the most.

Catherine and I stayed in touch all day, at distance: she followed my updates, I sent her videos and pieces of road. In the evening, when my body started responding again, I finally ate something properly and got back in balance.

At night, beyond tiredness, the mental side arrived too: return to Italy now near, the sensation that the Camino was about to close, and at the same time the desire not to lose the bonds born along the way. It was already clear that the journey did not end at the return flight: it was becoming a network of people to reconnect with over time.

**Sensory detail**
Real thirst, empty legs, salt on skin, lighthouse wind, and that mix of nausea and happiness that comes only after pushing effort to the very end.

**What I understood**
The most memorable endings are not the most comfortable ones: they are the ones where you arrive emptied out, but certain you lived the day exactly the way you wanted.

items.place: Hotel Congreso, Santiago de Compostela, Spain

days.notes:
**Title**
Return from Muxía and decompression day.

**Where I was / stage**
July 23: return to Santiago after Muxía, with a full recovery afternoon outside the city center.

**Key scene**
July 23 was the true “landing” day after the final effort. In the morning I shared in the group how the run to Muxía had gone: very tight arrival, strong heat, continuous alternation between running and walking, because I was exhausted by the blazing sun and extremely thirsty, lots of water right after arrival, and then passage at km 0 later at sunset. It was one of those satisfactions where you feel euphoria and full tiredness together.

Once back in Santiago, however, mode changed: full stop. Instead of staying in the center, I chose Hotel Congreso, about 5/6 km from Santiago. I got there by taxi specifically to avoid adding unnecessary kilometers: that day I only wanted to recover.

Afternoon plan: pool and spa. In chat I was telling Catherine I would spend the whole time there in total relax mode, and that is exactly what happened. Even when I found out the spa opened later than expected, I still kept the day slow: no pushing, no stages, just recovery.

During the day I talked with several people, but without logistical meetups: Laura and Juan had gone elsewhere, so I decided to go straight to the hotel and stay there until evening. Catherine and I kept messaging a lot, with irony, complicity, and light moments. At the same time, I was already focused on next-day logistics: St. James celebration in town, heavy security around, afternoon flight, and airport transfer with two buses (hotel → Santiago → airport).

The evening stayed fully relaxed, with the feeling I had earned that slowness. It was no longer time to “push”: it was time to let everything settle.

**Sensory detail**
Pool water, spa heat, white sheets, and body finally still after days of continuous effort.

**What I understood**
Recovery is also part of the Camino: it is not a break from the route, it is the final necessary piece to close it well.
recommendations: Hotel Congreso (Rúa das Mámoas de Montouto, Nº 4, 15894): very useful if you want a real recovery day, with pool/spa, outside the city-center rush. Link: https://maps.app.goo.gl/udKN6YJ71Lwxcx1X6?g_st=ic

days.notes:
**Title**
Last day: slow breakfast, final stamps, return.

**Where I was / stage**
July 24: journey closing day, from the hotel outside Santiago to departure through the airport.

**Key scene**
The day started at the hotel with a proper breakfast, the kind you enjoy without rushing: both sweet and savory. After weeks of pilgrim pace and basic meals, that moment felt different, almost like an official transition toward the return.

Even though I finally had a comfortable bed and could sleep longer, I still woke up very early: at 5, then at 6, then at 7. My body was still tuned to Camino rhythm.

After breakfast I went back to Santiago and stopped by the office to get the last sello. It was a simple gesture, but symbolic: the last stamp, the last formal piece before truly closing this adventure.

The rest of the day was mostly logistics: transfer to the airport, waiting, then departure with delay. We still exchanged messages in chat, with that mix of lightness and end-of-journey melancholy. In the group, farewell messages were passing around, with the shared feeling that the Camino was ending for everyone, each in their own way.

Right at boarding time, I also made a symbolic gesture: with my small knife I shortened the stick to make it fit Ryanair size limits. In reality, it had already become very short, so much so that I had not used it for walking for many days because it was worn down. But by then it was too full of stories to leave it there: it was my souvenir, rich with history and memories, between all my friends signatures collected on the Camino and the tally marks that recorded the passing days.

**Sensory detail**
A rich breakfast, airport noise, and that strange suspended calm when you know you are leaving a place that got inside you.

**What I understood**
The last day does not just close a stage: it puts order into everything you lived, and makes you understand what you truly want to take home.
